annotations.label: Variance

anchor.snippet: The variance of a random variable

annotations.note: `ProbabilityTheory.variance X μ = (∫⁻ ω, ‖X ω - μ[X]‖ₑ ^ 2 ∂μ).toReal` matches the Wikipedia definition Var[X] = E[(X - E[X])²].

anchors.snippet: The variance of a random variable

annotations.label: Variance as covariance with itself

anchor.snippet: The variance can also be thought of as the covariance of a random variable with itself

annotations.note: `covariance_self : cov[X, X; μ] = Var[X; μ]` directly captures this identity.

anchors.snippet: The variance can also be thought of as the covariance of a random variable with itself

annotations.label: Variance as second cumulant

anchor.snippet: The variance is also equivalent to the second cumulant of a probability distribution

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `cgf` (cumulant generating function) but has no named decl for individual cumulants κ_n, so the identity Var = κ₂ is not stated.

anchors.snippet: The variance is also equivalent to the second cumulant of a probability distribution

annotations.label: Computational formula for variance

anchor.snippet: the variance of

annotations.note: `variance_eq_sub` states `variance X μ = μ[X^2] - μ[X]^2` under `MemLp X 2 μ` and a probability measure.

anchors.snippet: the variance of

annotations.label: Variance of a discrete random variable

anchor.snippet: If the generator of random variable

annotations.note: The general `variance` specialises to the discrete case via a discrete (e.g. counting/Dirac) measure, but there is no dedicated discrete-variance decl.

anchors.snippet: If the generator of random variable

annotations.label: Variance of equally likely values

anchor.snippet: The variance of a collection of

annotations.note: No dedicated lemma for the variance of a uniform finite distribution `(1/n)∑(x_i - μ)²` in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The variance of a collection of

annotations.label: Pairwise-distance form of variance

anchor.snippet: can be equivalently expressed, without directly referring to the mean, in terms of squared deviations of all pairwise squared distances

annotations.note: No grep hit for the pairwise-distance identity `(1/(2n²))∑∑(x_i - x_j)²` in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: can be equivalently expressed, without directly referring to the mean, in terms of squared deviations of all pairwise squared distances

annotations.label: Variance of an absolutely continuous random variable

anchor.snippet: has a probability density function

annotations.note: Mathlib's variance is defined for any measure (including ones with densities), but there is no specific decl giving `∫(x - μ)² f(x) dx`.

anchors.snippet: has a probability density function

annotations.label: Variance via improper Riemann integral

anchor.snippet: is Riemann-integrable on every finite interval

annotations.note: No decl relates variance specifically to a Riemann improper integral.

anchors.snippet: is Riemann-integrable on every finite interval

annotations.label: Variance of the exponential distribution

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution with parameter

annotations.note: `Mathlib/Probability/Distributions/Exponential.lean` defines the exponential PDF but does not compute its variance.

anchors.snippet: The exponential distribution with parameter

annotations.label: Variance of a fair six-sided die

anchor.snippet: A fair six-sided die can be modeled as a discrete random variable

annotations.note: No die-specific variance example exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A fair six-sided die can be modeled as a discrete random variable

annotations.label: Variance of an n-sided die

anchor.snippet: The general formula for the variance of the outcome

annotations.note: The formula `(n² - 1)/12` for uniform-die variance is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: The general formula for the variance of the outcome

annotations.label: Non-negativity of variance

anchor.snippet: Variance is non-negative because the squares are positive or zero

annotations.note: `variance_nonneg : 0 ≤ variance X μ` is in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Variance is non-negative because the squares are positive or zero

annotations.label: Variance of a constant

anchor.snippet: The variance of a constant is zero.

annotations.note: `variance_zero : variance 0 μ = 0` covers the zero constant; no general `variance_const : Var[fun _ ↦ c] = 0` decl was found (but `variance_const_add`/`covariance_const_left` give the result indirectly).

anchors.snippet: The variance of a constant is zero.

annotations.label: Zero variance implies almost surely constant

anchor.snippet: if the variance of a random variable is 0, then it is almost surely a constant

annotations.note: `ae_eq_integral_of_variance_eq_zero` gives `∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, X ω = μ[X]` from `Var[X;μ] = 0`.

anchors.snippet: if the variance of a random variable is 0, then it is almost surely a constant

annotations.label: Finiteness of variance

anchor.snippet: If a distribution does not have a finite expected value

annotations.note: `evariance_lt_top_iff_memLp` characterises finite variance as `MemLp X 2 μ`; the Wikipedia discussion of pathological non-finite moments is not phrased exactly.

annotations.label: Law of total variance

anchor.snippet: The general formula for variance decomposition or the law of total variance

annotations.note: `integral_condVar_add_variance_condExp` states `μ[Var[X;μ|m]] + Var[μ[X|m]; μ] = Var[X; μ]`, the law of total variance.

anchors.snippet: The general formula for variance decomposition or the law of total variance

annotations.label: Variance from the CDF

anchor.snippet: The population variance for a non-negative random variable can be expressed in terms of the cumulative distribution function

annotations.note: No grep hit relating variance to a CDF-based integral.

anchors.snippet: The population variance for a non-negative random variable can be expressed in terms of the cumulative distribution function

annotations.label: Characteristic property of variance

anchor.snippet: The second moment of a random variable attains the minimum value when taken around the first moment

annotations.note: No decl stating that `c ↦ E[(X - c)²]` is minimised at `c = E[X]` was found.

annotations.label: Translation invariance of variance

anchor.section: Addition and multiplication by a constant
anchor.snippet: Variance is invariant with respect to changes in a location parameter

annotations.note: `variance_add_const : Var[fun ω ↦ X ω + c; μ] = Var[X; μ]` (with `variance_const_add` and `variance_sub_const` siblings).

anchors.section: Addition and multiplication by a constant
anchors.snippet: Variance is invariant with respect to changes in a location parameter

annotations.label: Scaling of variance by a constant

anchor.section: Addition and multiplication by a constant
anchor.snippet: If all values are scaled by a constant, the variance is scaled by the square of that constant

annotations.note: `variance_const_mul : variance (fun ω => c * X ω) μ = c^2 * variance X μ`.

anchors.section: Addition and multiplication by a constant
anchors.snippet: If all values are scaled by a constant, the variance is scaled by the square of that constant

anchor.section: Addition and multiplication by a constant
anchor.snippet: The variance of a sum of two random variables is given by

annotations.note: `variance_add : Var[X + Y; μ] = Var[X; μ] + 2 * cov[X, Y; μ] + Var[Y; μ]`.

anchors.section: Addition and multiplication by a constant
anchors.snippet: The variance of a sum of two random variables is given by

annotations.label: Uncorrelated random variables

anchor.snippet: then they are said to be uncorrelated

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses uncorrelatedness as `cov[X, Y; μ] = 0` (or `μ[X * Y] = μ[X] * μ[Y]`) but has no named `Uncorrelated` definition.

anchors.snippet: then they are said to be uncorrelated

anchor.snippet: if the random variables

annotations.note: `variance_sum` reduces to `∑ Var[X_i]` when off-diagonal covariances vanish, but there is no dedicated uncorrelated-hypothesis form; `IndepFun.variance_sum` covers the stronger independent case.

annotations.label: Independence implies uncorrelated

anchor.snippet: Since independent random variables are always uncorrelated

annotations.note: `IndepFun.covariance_eq_zero` shows `X ⟂ᵢ[μ] Y → cov[X, Y; μ] = 0` under `MemLp` hypotheses.

annotations.label: Bienaymé formula

anchor.snippet: This statement is called the Bienaymé formula

annotations.note: `IndepFun.variance_sum : Var[∑ X_i] = ∑ Var[X_i]` for pairwise independent variables — the Bienaymé identity (under stronger independence).

anchors.snippet: This statement is called the Bienaymé formula

annotations.label: Variance of sum equals sum of covariances

anchor.snippet: the variance of the sum of n variables is the sum of their covariances

annotations.note: `variance_sum : Var[∑ i, X i; μ] = ∑ i, ∑ j, cov[X i, X j; μ]` is exactly this identity.
